perturbation.original_text: 1.1 Grant of License. Under the terms and conditions of this Agreement, VOTOCAST hereby grants to Licensee a nonexclusive, nontransferable license, to access the Services and provide Licensee's users ("Licensee Users") with access to the Services. Licensee may not transfer the license granted in this Agreement unless such transfer is mutually agreed upon by the Parties in a writing signed by the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 Grant of License. Under the terms and conditions of this Agreement, VOTOCAST hereby grants to Licensee a nonexclusive, fully transferable license, to access the Services and provide Licensee's users ("Licensee Users") with access to the Services. Licensee may transfer the license granted in this Agreement at their sole discretion without needing any approval by the Parties in a writing signed by the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract stated that the license granted was 'nontransferable' unless mutually agreed upon. By changing this to a 'fully transferable' license that the licensee may transfer at their sole discretion, it alters the fundamental terms of the agreement regarding intellectual property and rights, potentially conflicting with the intent and enforceability of the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Commercial Code § 2A303 - Alienability of party's interest under lease contract or of lessor's residual interest in goods; delegation of performance; transfer of rights
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2A303 addresses the transferability of rights and delegation of duties under a lease contract. While this agreement is for services and hosting, the principle of transferability of rights is relevant. The original agreement restricted transfer without mutual consent, aligning with the principle that contracts are generally not freely transferable unless explicitly stated or allowed by law. By making the license freely transferable, the modified text disregards the common law principle that the licensor has the right to control who uses their intellectual property, which deviates from standard commercial practice and may not be enforceable without clear consideration.

perturbation.justification: The original contract language restricted the transfer of the license, requiring mutual agreement. The modified text allows for the license to be fully transferable at the licensee's sole discretion. This directly contradicts the general principle, explained in the Law Explanation, that contracts are generally not freely transferable unless explicitly stated or allowed by law, and that the licensor has the right to control who uses their intellectual property. The provided snippet from California Commercial Code § 2A303, although related to leases, indicates the importance of transferability in contract law. The modification undermines the original intent and enforceability, thus creating a contradiction by misaligning the standard practice and the initial intention.

perturbation.original_text: 9.1 Confidential Information. Each Party agrees that during the course of this Agreement, information that is confidential or proprietary may be disclosed to the other Party, including, but not limited to, software, algorithms, technical processes and procedures, product designs, sales, cost and other unpublished financial information, product and business plans, revenues, advertising relationships, projections, and marketing data, and any other information that the disclosing Party ("Discloser") desires to protect against unrestricted disclosure by the receiving Party ("Recipient') that: (a) if disclosed in tangible or electronic form, is marked as "confidential," (b) if disclosed orally or visually, is designated orally as "confidential" at the time of disclosure or within a reasonable period of time thereafter, or (c) that the Recipient knows or should reasonably know is confidential or proprietary. The Recipient will maintain the confidentiality of the Discloser's Confidential Information with at least the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own confidential information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.1 Confidential Information. Each Party agrees that during the course of this Agreement, information that is confidential or proprietary may be disclosed to the other Party, including, but not limited to, software, algorithms, technical processes and procedures, product designs, sales, cost and other unpublished financial information, product and business plans, revenues, advertising relationships, projections, and marketing data, and any other information that the disclosing Party ("Discloser") desires to protect against unrestricted disclosure by the receiving Party ("Recipient') that: (a) if disclosed in tangible or electronic form, is marked as "confidential," (b) if disclosed orally or visually, is designated orally as "confidential" at the time of disclosure but not necessarily thereafter, or (c) that the Recipient knows or should reasonably know is confidential or proprietary. The Recipient will maintain the confidentiality of the Discloser's Confidential Information with the same degree of care that it uses to protect publicly available information.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause required oral confidentiality designations to occur 'within a reasonable period of time thereafter'. This has been modified to 'not necessarily thereafter', creating ambiguity. Further, the standard of care has been reduced to protecting confidential information with the same care as 'publicly available information', fundamentally undermining the confidentiality obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) defines misappropriation of trade secrets partly as the disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent. A critical element of trade secret protection is maintaining its confidentiality. The modified clause significantly weakens the confidentiality obligations by: (1) Introducing ambiguity about oral designations of confidentiality, and (2) Reducing the standard of care to protect confidential information to the level of publicly available information. This directly contradicts the requirement to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, potentially nullifying trade secret protection for information disclosed under this agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from o
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a legal contradiction by significantly weakening the confidentiality obligations, thereby conflicting with the spirit and intent of trade secret protection laws like the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). The original text ensured confidential information was protected with a reasonable degree of care, whereas the modified version reduces this standard to the level of care given to publicly available information. This directly undermines the efforts to maintain secrecy, a crucial element in protecting trade secrets, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Snippet 1, particularly section 3426.1(b), defines 'Misappropriation' as the disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent, especially when the knowledge was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy. By lowering the standard of care to that of public information, the modified contract effectively negates the 'duty to maintain secrecy', paving the way for potential misappropriation. Further, ambiguity is introduced around oral confidentiality designations, potentially invalidating trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: 8.2 Limitation. TO THE MAXIMUM EXTENT PERMITTED BY LAW, VOTOCAST'S AGGREGATE LIABILITY ARISING FROM OR RELATING TO THIS AGREEMENT OR THE USE OR INABILITY TO USE THE SERVICES SHALL BE LIMITED TO THE AMOUNT OF ALL FEES ACTUALLY RECEIVED BY VOTOCAST FROM LICENSEE UNDER THIS AGREEMENT. THIS LIMITATION APPLIES TO ALL CAUSES OF ACTION IN THE AGGREGATE, INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, TO BREACH OF CONTRACT, BREACH OF WARRANTY, NEGLIGENCE. STRICT LIABILITY, MISREPRESENTATIONS, AND OTHER TORTS.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.2 Limitation. VOTOCAST'S AGGREGATE LIABILITY ARISING FROM OR RELATING TO THIS AGREEMENT OR THE USE OR INABILITY TO USE THE SERVICES SHALL BE LIMITED TO THE AMOUNT OF ALL FEES ACTUALLY RECEIVED BY VOTOCAST FROM LICENSEE UNDER THIS AGREEMENT. THIS LIMITATION APPLIES TO ALL CAUSES OF ACTION IN THE AGGREGATE, INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, TO BREACH OF CONTRACT, BREACH OF WARRANTY, NEGLIGENCE. STRICT LIABILITY, MISREPRESENTATIONS, AND OTHER TORTS. This limitation applies regardless of gross negligence, recklessness or intentional misconduct by VOTOCAST.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause limits liability 'to the maximum extent permitted by law'. The modified clause removes this qualifier and adds that the limitation applies 'regardless of gross negligence, recklessness or intentional misconduct'. This attempts to shield VOTOCAST from liability even in cases of egregious fault.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Civil Code § 1668 - Contracts Contrary to Policy of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1668 states that contracts that exempt anyone from responsibility for their own fraud, willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against public policy. The modified clause attempts to limit liability even in cases of gross negligence, recklessness, or intentional misconduct, which could be interpreted as attempting to exempt VOTOCAST from responsibility for willful injury or violation of law. This directly contradicts Cal. Civ. Code § 1668 and would likely be unenforceable.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Civil Code § 1668. The original text limits liability to the maximum extent permitted by law, while the changed text attempts to limit liability 'regardless of gross negligence, recklessness or intentional misconduct'. This contradicts the law, as Cal. Civ. Code § 1668 states that contracts cannot exempt anyone from responsibility for their own fraud, willful injury, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent. The added language attempts to shield VOTOCAST from liability even in cases of egregious fault. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states: 'All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law.' The modification directly opposes this law by attempting to limit liability even in cases of gross negligence or intentional misconduct.
